title: Repository Repair, Release, and Collaboration Capstone · Graph Retrieval
summary: Recover and ship a repository containing mixed work, a conflict, a mistaken commit, a remote update, and a release deadline. Learners retrieve rules and procedures before seeing feedback. Planned artifact: Extend the evidence record for a complete Git foundations evidence portfolio after a delayed changed case. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Capture repository boundaries, config origins, current operation, worktree/index state, graph, remotes, and recovery refs before repair.
Order preservation, reproduction, local repair, tests, history cleanup, and publication from least to most irreversible.
Coordinate rewritten history, conflicts, ownership, release changes, and recovery instructions with explicit affected refs and people.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# git-basics cumulative repository evidence notebook
# Git operations change only disposable simulated repository state.
# Inspect status, diffs, graph, refs, and recovery before every mutation.

steps.title: Separate evidence from confidence
steps.instruction:
review case — A review repository incident combines mixed work, divergent refs, one conflict, an incorrect public commit, and release v4.0. Preserve recovery refs, repair the least destructive layer, and verify graph plus tree.

Inspect the Handoff Runbook trace for this review case. Which record connects a precondition, observed state change, and boundary result?
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for publish an accessible repository runbook covering setup, workflow, gates, release, recovery, and escalation with tested commands.

content.text: For Handoff Runbook, distinguish a claim from evidence by locating the relevant precondition, trace or invariant, and changed input result.

hints: Name the Handoff Runbook precondition or invariant that the review case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: A review repository incident combines mixed work, divergent refs, one conflict, an incorrect public commit, and release v4.0. Preserve recovery refs, repair the least destructive layer, and verify graph plus tree.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: Experienced contributors do not need repository-specific recovery documentation.

stimulus.title: Handoff Runbook case record
stimulus.caption: Three excerpts from a learner evidence packet; only one connects a claim to a reproducible check.

lines.label: assumption
lines.text: A review repository incident combines mixed work, divergent refs, one conflict, an incorrect public commit, and release v4.0. Preserve recovery refs, repair the least destructive layer, and verify graph plus tree.

lines.label: risk
lines.text: Experienced contributors do not need repository-specific recovery documentation.

lines.label: evidence
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Publish an accessible repository runbook covering setup, workflow, gates, release, recovery, and escalation with tested commands. Artifact under review: a complete Git foundations evidence portfolio.

options.text: The record names the assumption, applies Handoff Runbook, and reports a changed-case check tied to the stakeholder decision.
The record says the result looks right but supplies no quantity, trace, condition, or counterexample.
Apply the visible technique immediately, omit assumptions, and treat one successful output as complete proof.

steps.title: Retrieve the complete evidence cycle
steps.instruction: Order the Repository Triage evidence cycle for this review task before implementing it.
steps.why: Correct order keeps Repository Triage assumptions visible before execution and verifies its result against a boundary case afterward.

content.text: A review repository incident combines mixed work, divergent refs, one conflict, an incorrect public commit, and release v5.0. Preserve recovery refs, repair the least destructive layer, and verify graph plus tree.

content.title: Ordering constraint
content.text: Order work so Repository Triage has a declared contract before execution and a boundary or counterexample after it.

hints: Name the Repository Triage precondition or invariant that the review case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: A review repository incident combines mixed work, divergent refs, one conflict, an incorrect public commit, and release v5.0. Preserve recovery refs, repair the least destructive layer, and verify graph plus tree.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: git status alone captures all state needed for repository recovery.

options.text: Inspect repository boundary, config origin, current operation, worktree, index, refs, and graph
Preserve uncommitted work and a recovery ref, then state intended tree and topology
Preview and perform the least destructive simulated Git operation
Verify status, staged and range diffs, graph, tests, remote target, and recovery path

steps.title: Record what required effort
steps.instruction: Write the review evidence note for Handoff Runbook: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A review repository incident combines mixed work, divergent refs, one conflict, an incorrect public commit, and release v6.0. Preserve recovery refs, repair the least destructive layer, and verify graph plus tree.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Publish an accessible repository runbook covering setup, workflow, gates, release, recovery, and escalation with tested commands.

hints: Name the Handoff Runbook precondition or invariant that the review case must preserve.

Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: Experienced contributors do not need repository-specific recovery documentation.
